am puzzled on why `eltype` does not infer more precisely for `Iterators.map(f, iterators...)`, especially when both the `eltype` of the input iterators and the return type of `f` seem to be inferable.
Would it be possible to improve the `eltype` inference for `Iterators`.

```julia
julia> fun(x) = x^2
fun (generic function with 1 method)

julia> a = Iterators.map(fun, 1:3)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, typeof(fun)}(fun, 1:3)

julia> eltype(a)
Any    # Expected: Int
```

This enhancement could help improve type stability and make `Iterators.map` more consistent with the inferred types of other Julia constructs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

am puzzled on why `eltype` does not infer more precisely for `Iterators.map(f, iterators...)`, especially when both the `eltype` of the input iterators and the return type of `f` seem to be inferable.
Would it be possible to improve the `eltype` inference for `Iterators`.

```julia
julia> fun(x) = x^2
fun (generic function with 1 method)

julia> a = Iterators.map(fun, 1:3)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, typeof(fun)}(fun, 1:3)

julia> eltype(a)
Any    # Expected: Int
```

This enhancement could help improve type stability and make `Iterators.map` more consistent with the inferred types of other Julia constructs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2559987045","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891#issuecomment-2559987045","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891","id":2559987045,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Ylk1l","user":{"login":"ehehela","id":56829479,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2ODI5NDc5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56829479?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela","html_url":"https://github.com/ehehela","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ehehela/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-23T16:26:55Z","updated_at":"2024-12-23T16:26:55Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Thanks for your detailed explanation.
According to your answer,  it seems that overloading `eltype` for user-defined iterators is not essential for performance. Is that correct?
Additionally, I noticed there is an issue (#48249) discussing the need for accurate `eltype` on `Iterators.flatten`.

Does anyone know what’s going on?

```
using LinearAlgebra

abstract type GeoShape{dim} end
struct GSPolyline <: GeoShape{2} end
struct GSPolygon <: GeoShape{2} end

struct Geometry{dim,T,S<:GeoShape{dim}}
    coords::Vector{NTuple{dim,T}}
end
Polyline{T} = Geometry{2,T,GSPolyline}
Polyline(coords::Vector{NTuple{2,T}}) where {T} = Polyline{T}(coords)

function signed_tangentdistance(pline::Polyline, querypt)
    plcoor = pline.coords
    return max(signed_tangentdistance(plcoor[begin], plcoor[begin+1], querypt), signed_tangentdistance(plcoor[end], plcoor[end-1], querypt))
end
function signed_tangentdistance(point1, point2, querypt)
    line_vec = point1 .- point2
    line_vec = line_vec ./ √(sum(line_vec .^ 2))
    point_vec = querypt .- point1
    t = point_vec ⋅ line_vec
    return t
end

using Plots

pline = Polyline{Float64}([(1, 1), (2, 2), (4, 2), (4, 4), (2, 5), (4, 6), (6, 4), (6, 1), (5, 2)])
x = range(0.0, 7.0, length=1000)
p = contour(x, x, (x, y) -> signed_tangentdistance(pline, (x, y)); fill=true, lw=0, color=:RdBu, aspect_ratio=:equal,clabels=true)
display(p)
```

> Please submit a bug report with steps to reproduce this fault, and any error messages that follow (in their entirety). Thanks.

Does anyone know what’s going on?

```
using LinearAlgebra

abstract type GeoShape{dim} end
struct GSPolyline <: GeoShape{2} end
struct GSPolygon <: GeoShape{2} end

struct Geometry{dim,T,S<:GeoShape{dim}}
    coords::Vector{NTuple{dim,T}}
end
Polyline{T} = Geometry{2,T,GSPolyline}
Polyline(coords::Vector{NTuple{2,T}}) where {T} = Polyline{T}(coords)

function signed_tangentdistance(pline::Polyline, querypt)
    plcoor = pline.coords
    return max(signed_tangentdistance(plcoor[begin], plcoor[begin+1], querypt), signed_tangentdistance(plcoor[end], plcoor[end-1], querypt))
end
function signed_tangentdistance(point1, point2, querypt)
    line_vec = point1 .- point2
    line_vec = line_vec ./ √(sum(line_vec .^ 2))
    point_vec = querypt .- point1
    t = point_vec ⋅ line_vec
    return t
end

using Plots

pline = Polyline{Float64}([(1, 1), (2, 2), (4, 2), (4, 4), (2, 5), (4, 6), (6, 4), (6, 1), (5, 2)])
x = range(0.0, 7.0, length=1000)
p = contour(x, x, (x, y) -> signed_tangentdistance(pline, (x, y)); fill=true, lw=0, color=:RdBu, aspect_ratio=:equal,clabels=true)
display(p)
```
